title: Negotiating an End to the War in Iran
content:
Mass protests in Iran over a collapsing currency, shortages, and joblessness highlighted the regime's profound weakness before the war began. American and Israeli bombings have only worsened these conditions, making the regime even more desperate for sanctions relief. However, the success of the current blockade is highly uncertain, as such measures usually take months or years to force compliance. The regime knows this is its best chance to strike a bargain, meaning it might endure the blockade longer than anticipated. What matters most now is whether ongoing talks can produce a viable, long-term deal.

The first stage of negotiations must focus on reopening the Strait of Hormuz, a critical passageway for global shipping. In no circumstance should America agree to let Iran levy tolls on ships traversing these international waters. Doing so would give Iran a permanent hold over the rest of the region and threaten global trade. However, America may have to trade the lifting of some sanctions in return for guaranteed safe passage. The second stage of talks will center on Iran’s nuclear program, which will be fiendishly complex to resolve.

Both sides currently lack trust, making them hesitant to take bold steps fearful the other will not honor the deal. America wants Iran's stock of enriched uranium removed and a total ban on fresh enrichment activities. Meanwhile, Iran views its enrichment capabilities as a symbol of sovereignty that it is highly reluctant to surrender. Compromises are available, such as diluting the uranium for civilian use or limiting the number of active centrifuges. Even with an agreement, Iran will remain a threatening presence, requiring significant work to rebuild the region's security infrastructure.
